scene.title: Picking a tennis hitting partner
scene.summary: A club tennis player deciding which of two mid-level partners to book regular courts with.

extras.friendship_context: Weekly court time is observable; committed partners must match hours.

records.scenario_text: At the Riverside Tennis Club, you're debating who to partner with for weekly sessions. Booking courts requires matching commitment—both hours and fees add up. For you, tennis is more than exercise; it's a cornerstone of your week that strengthens real connections. But $45 hourly court fees mean adjusting your budget—you've started packing lunches instead of eating out to cover costs. First option is Mia, a consistent baseline player. She radiates enthusiasm about tennis bonds, calling them 'essential for mental reset and real friendships.' Yet she hesitates before confirming sessions, later admitting she skips streaming subscriptions to afford it. Second is Jake, whose powerful serve complements your net game. He sees real value in teaming up, saying 'regular play could make our friendship click.' But his jaw tightens discussing fees; he once canceled last minute when rent was due, confessing 'some weeks this competes with groceries.' Court reservations open tomorrow. Staring at both names, you wonder—whose rhythm and reality truly sync with yours?

scene.title: Picking a regular running partner
scene.summary: A runner new to a Saturday run club deciding who to pair up with for midweek runs.

extras.friendship_context: Running mileage is the visible trait; sustainable friendship needs matching weekly load.

records.scenario_text: After joining the local Saturday run club, Alex felt energized but wanted more consistency. Midweek runs with a dedicated partner could make those lonely miles fly by. For Alex, this wasn’t just exercise—it was about forging a meaningful bond that truly enriched life. Yet every running expense, from shoes to hydration packs, tugged uncomfortably against rent and utility payments back home. Two potential partners emerged: Mia, a cheerful regular who mentioned casually that pairing up sounded 'pleasant enough' during chats. She’d shrugged off new gear costs recently, noting they 'slid right into the budget.' Then there was Jake, who’d proactively shared how much he valued structured accountability, calling it 'useful for staying grounded.' His recent trail-run investments came without visible strain—'easy adjustments,' he’d texted. Alex lingered after the next Saturday session, eyes darting between them. Mia offered lightness; Jake promised substance. Both could keep pace, but whose rhythm matched Alex’s blend of deep commitment and stretched wallet? The choice loomed: invest limited energy with Mia’s breezy approach, or Jake’s steadier pull?

scene.summary: A solo founder at a Friday founder dinner choosing who to add as a weekly accountability buddy.

extras.friendship_context: Deep-work cadence is observable; only matching cadences sustain the pairing.

records.scenario_text: At the weekly founder dinner, Alex scanned the room for an accountability partner to share weekly deep-work hours. As a solo founder, Alex knew this commitment needed mutual dedication—observable cadence was key to lasting. He valued the partnership clearly; it would anchor his productivity without straining finances. His comfortable budget made the coworking fees trivial. Two candidates stood out: Jake (option A) voiced genuine enthusiasm, saying shared focus 'would genuinely elevate his workflow'—a clear value match. But Jake's startup was bootstrapped; he mentioned how the expense 'competed with essential server costs,' leaving him visibly stressed about bills. Meanwhile, Lena (option B) leaned in eagerly, calling the pact 'a career game-changer worth every minute'—her conviction radiating strong worth. Yet Lena hesitated slightly, admitting she'd 'need to skip restaurant meals' to afford it, her budget requiring conscious adjustments. Both understood the stakes, but Alex wavered: Jake’s financial strain versus Lena’s fervent belief. Who could sustain the rhythm when life tightened?

But when we floated a picnic idea, Sofia hesitated, explaining how even $15 park outings compete with their medical bills. Both families extend warmth, but committing to weekly rhythms feels precarious. Do we prioritize easygoing comfort with the Carters or meaningful connection with the Riveras, knowing our own budget tightrope?

records.scenario_text: Freshly settled in Boston after the move, I'm navigating the social scene alone. At a Friday co-working meetup downtown, I connected with two potential friends: Jake, a graphic designer who just relocated too, and Mia, a local nurse. We bonded over casual chats about exploring the city through monthly leisure spend—dinners, concerts, or museum trips that add up fast in USD. For me, building this friendship would be pleasant but not life-changing; it's a mild interest that fits around my edges. Still, I'm budget-conscious about it—every outing means skipping Uber rides or cooking more meals at home to balance the spending. Jake clearly values deepening connections, mentioning how reliable friendships help him feel grounded in a new city. But he's financially constrained; last week he hesitated over concert tickets, admitting leisure expenses compete with his student loan payments. Mia radiates enthusiasm—she called regular meetups vital for joy after long hospital shifts, showing strong value in bonding. She's also budget-conscious, though, swapping brunch spots for picnics to afford our plans. Now I'm lingering over coffee, unsure whether to commit to Jake or Mia for these monthly rituals.

Now, sipping cold brew alone, Alex weighs who to invite for next month's outing. Maya promises deeper connection but financial strain, while Ben mirrors Alex's casual outlook yet faces real hardship. Which rhythm feels sustainable?

records.scenario_text:
Freshly transplanted to Boston, Alex scanned the co-working space for potential close friendships. Last Friday's meetup highlighted two candidates: Priya, a warm illustrator who laughed easily, and Marcus, a thoughtful data analyst with dry humor. The proposed ritual? A monthly leisure spend (USD) on concerts or neighborhood exploring. For Alex, the outlay felt effortless—a minor line item in a stable budget—while the companionship promised casual enjoyment, nothing transformative. 

Priya radiated enthusiasm about bonding, calling it 'exactly the connection I've been craving' after her recent breakup. Yet her eyes clouded discussing expenses; she juggled freelance checks and medical debt, admitting even modest outings meant sacrificing groceries. Marcus offered polite interest, shrugging that shared meals would 'pass the time pleasantly enough.' But his tightened jaw revealed identical financial strain—contract work left him rationing every dollar between rent and loans. 

Now Alex wavered. Priya's heartfelt yearning tugged at them, yet her monetary stress mirrored Marcus's grim reality. Meanwhile, Marcus's low-key vibe aligned with Alex's own outlook... but could ambivalence sustain a ritual? Choosing who to build this monthly rhythm with felt impossible.

records.scenario_text: Jamie studied the club noticeboard, needing to choose a weekly tennis partner. Both options seemed promising: Elena, who always brought fierce energy to their trial sessions, and Ben, whose steady backhand impressed during drills. For Jamie, these matches meant more than exercise—they were anchors in a hectic week, moments where camaraderie and competition fused into something genuinely fulfilling. Yet booking courts demanded tradeoffs; Jamie had started packing lunches to afford the fees, knowing each hour meant sacrificing other small pleasures. Elena mirrored that intensity, calling their potential partnership 'essential' for her wellbeing between work stresses, though she’d admitted last week that court costs sometimes meant delaying her car payment. Ben, equally passionate about building a reliable tennis ritual, casually mentioned the expense barely registered against his stable income—he’d even offered to cover extra balls without a second thought. Now, pen hovering over the sign-up sheet, Jamie wavered. Both valued the connection deeply, but their financial realities clashed. Who could share the commitment without strain creeping into their rallies?

Comfort or kinship through shared budgeting?

But grabbing those $15 tickets or $25 meals means rethinking weekend treats; we'd skip streaming subscriptions to afford it. Then we met two possibilities. First, the Garcias: Leo and Rosa beam when describing how these gatherings would become their family's emotional cornerstone, calling it 'non-negotiable joy.' Yet they track every dollar too, opting for parks over theme parks to stretch their budget. Second, the Parkers: Tom and Priya radiate equal fervor, saying weekly meetups are 'what community is built on,' but Tom admitted quietly that even splitting a $20 museum fee forces hard choices between outings and their daughter's music lessons. Now I linger over texts to both families—who can we commit to without financial strain or faded enthusiasm derailing this fragile tradition?

records.scenario_text: Taylor joined the Saturday run club last month and now wants a reliable partner for midweek miles. For Taylor, these runs would add a pleasant social rhythm without becoming life's centerpiece—a nice-to-have, not a must-have. But the costs hit hard: new shoes and gym fees competed with rent payments, making every dollar a careful calculation. At the club, two options stood out. Alex saw the runs as a casual bonus—a light commitment that'd fit neatly around other plans. Though financially mindful, Alex could manage the expenses by swapping coffee-shop stops for home-brews. Then there was Jamie, who radiated genuine enthusiasm. To Jamie, this partnership meant accountability and connection, something worth prioritizing weekly. Jamie also watched spending closely, budgeting carefully to afford decent gear without hardship. Taylor weighed both: Alex matched the low-stakes vibe but not the money stress, while Jamie's zeal felt inspiring yet mismatched to Taylor's financial reality. Who could build a lasting routine without strain?

extras.friendship_context: Commitment hours are visible; mutual stability requires matching load.

records.scenario_text: At the monthly diaspora meetup, Sam scanned the room for a language-exchange partner. Reconnecting with their heritage through weekly practice felt deeply important – a commitment they valued immensely. Financially, Sam felt at ease; café sessions or textbook costs fit comfortably within their budget. Two candidates stood out: Mia, who glowed discussing shared cultural roots. She saw these exchanges as vital, her eyes lighting up at the thought. Like Sam, her finances seemed unstrained; she casually mentioned grabbing dinner after sessions without hesitation. Then there was Jake, who offered a relaxed smile. He found the idea pleasant enough – a nice weekly diversion – but admitted it wouldn't be central to his routine. His spending flexibility mirrored the others'; he shrugged off talk of expenses, calling them 'no big deal.' Now Sam wavered: Mia promised profound connection, but was that intensity sustainable? Jake offered low-pressure camaraderie, yet would his mild enthusiasm fade? Choosing a weekly partner felt like picking a path where mutual dedication mattered most.

scene.title: Picking a tennis hitting partner
scene.summary: A club tennis player deciding which of two mid-level partners to book regular courts with.

extras.friendship_context: Weekly court time is observable; committed partners must match hours.

records.scenario_text: At Riverbend Tennis Club, I needed to lock in a weekly hitting partner before league signups closed. My own situation was tricky: I genuinely looked forward to these sessions—they anchored my week and helped shake off work stress—but each court hour meant skipping meals out or delaying car repairs. Jake, a fellow mid-level player I'd chatted with after drills, seemed deeply invested; he'd texted twice about 'making this a real commitment' and called our potential matches 'essential for his sanity'. Yet he'd also mentioned rationing sessions due to his daycare payments. Meanwhile Mia from accounting, while friendly during our trial hit, treated tennis more like casual entertainment—she once joked she'd 'just binge shows instead' if scheduling got tough—though she too confessed the fees strained her budget after rent hikes. With both needing consistent hours but Jake viewing it as crucial while Mia saw it as optional fun, I stared at the booking sheet wondering whose expectations would align with mine without breaking the bank.

scene.title: Picking a small-group partner at a new church
scene.summary: A newly-joined member of a church small-group weighing two potential regulars.

extras.friendship_context: Volunteer-hour commitment level is observable and determines matching stability.

records.scenario_text: After joining St. Mark's small group, Jamie weighed two volunteers for weekly outreach hours. For Jamie, the connection would be pleasant but not life-changing—a nice addition to their routine. Yet even modest commitments required juggling: skipping lunch out to cover bus fares or putting off a phone upgrade. Elena, a soft-spoken librarian, seemed similarly casual about deepening ties; she'd mentioned the hours were 'a nice way to unwind' and never stressed about expenses, once casually noting she 'barely noticed' the parking fees. Marcus, though, lit up discussing the work—calling it 'meaningful for building real community'—but often rushed off to his second job. He'd sighed about 'choosing between volunteer gas money and his kid's soccer fees' last month. Now Jamie stared at the sign-up sheet, pencil hovering: Elena's ease felt comfortable, but Marcus's genuine spark pulled harder. Who could share this rhythm without one of them straining?

scene.title: Picking a new close-friend after a move to Boston
scene.summary: A young professional who just moved to Boston is weighing two candidates from a Friday co-working meetup.

extras.friendship_context: Two candidates visibly differ in leisure-spend profile; only one matches.

records.scenario_text: Fresh off the plane in Boston, I scrolled through co-working meetup photos—there stood Leo and Maya from Friday's event. Both suggested grabbing dinner monthly, but $75 leisure spends already gnawed at my budget. Rent hikes meant every dollar counted, yet forging real connections felt vital here. Leo mentioned offhand he enjoyed casual hangouts; our chats were easy but surface-level. When I floated sushi plans, he hesitated: 'Maybe split appetizers? My car payment's due.' Maya's eyes lit up discussing shared museum trips—'Art feeds the soul,' she'd said—but later confessed skipping lunches to afford her student loans. Last week, she texted: 'Monthly culture fund: $75?'. My phone now shows both names. Leo's company would be light fun without strain, but Maya sparks that deep resonance I crave—even if ramen dinners follow. Who do I commit to without drowning financially?

records.scenario_text: Alex stared at the club's scheduling sheet, weighing two options for a weekly hitting partner. Tennis meant everything to Alex—those court hours anchored the week, a non-negotiable ritual that felt deeply worthwhile emotionally. But booking regular slots strained the budget severely; every dollar spent competed with rent payments, turning each session into a financial tradeoff. Mia seemed ideal: she'd emphasized how vital a committed partner was, texting, 'This connection could really elevate my game—it's important.' No problem, fits my lifestyle comfortably.' Now Alex hesitated: Mia mirrored the intensity but amplified money stress, while Jake eased the wallet yet made the bond feel incidental. Which partnership could sustain without snapping?

scene.title: Picking a regular running partner
scene.summary: A runner new to a Saturday run club deciding who to pair up with for midweek runs.

extras.friendship_context: Running mileage is the visible trait; sustainable friendship needs matching weekly load.

records.scenario_text: Alex joined the Saturday run club last month, scanning the group for someone to share midweek miles. For Alex, the partnership felt like a pleasant addition rather than a core commitment—a mild interest where the connection would be nice but not life-changing. Money-wise, Alex was budget-conscious; grabbing new running shoes or driving to trails meant skipping coffee runs. Among the regulars stood Ben, who saw real value in training together. For Ben, building this running bond wasn't just casual—it mattered deeply for motivation. Yet he faced tight finances; even small gear purchases competed with his rent. Then there was Chloe, radiating enthusiasm. She believed strongly in the friendship's worth—it felt vital and rewarding—though she too weighed expenses carefully, saying each tank of gas meant cutting back on dinners out. Staring at his worn sneakers, Alex wondered: Ben’s dedication was compelling, but could they sustain it with money so tight? Chloe’s energy was infectious, yet would her expectations clash with his own modest goals? Choosing who to sync weekly miles with felt like picking which path might unravel first.

records.scenario_text: As the newest member of Grace Community's small group, I needed to choose a weekly volunteer partner soon. Mia, a soft-spoken librarian, thought serving together would be a pleasant way to connect—something nice but not essential to her week. When she mentioned adjusting her grocery budget to afford the bus fare, I recognized that familiar tradeoff. Jake, a cheerful graphic designer, shared Mia's casual enthusiasm about building the habit, calling it 'a chill bonus' to his routine rather than a cornerstone. But unlike either of us, Jake shrugged off the transit costs—his recent promotion left him financially comfortable enough that the expenses barely registered. For me, this commitment held clear value; it wasn't just hours logged but a chance to root myself meaningfully in this community. Still, as someone juggling rent and utilities, I felt every dollar spent required careful shuffling of other priorities. Staring at the sign-up sheet, I wavered: Would Mia's similar budget reality make us more consistent, or would Jake's easy flexibility lead to steadier volunteering?

records.scenario_text: Alex and Jamie Miller, new parents in Oakridge, wanted to establish weekly family outings with neighborhood friends. For them, forming connections brought mild interest; the tie would be pleasant but not central to their lives. Financially, they were comfortable; the spending on zoo trips or pizzeria dinners fit easily into their budget. They considered two families. The first were the Parkers—Dan and Priya—down Elm Street. They saw clear value in neighbor bonds; the tie would matter and be useful for childcare swaps. Yet they were budget-conscious; the spending required some tradeoffs, like skipping movie nights to afford ice-skating. The second were the Reynolds, Mark and Elena, around the corner. They too found clear value in camaraderie; the tie would matter and be useful for sharing parenting tips. But they faced meaningful financial constraint; the spending competed with important bills, making mini-golf fees feel significant. Alex and Jamie wavered. Should they build the weekly ritual with the Parkers, who matched their enthusiasm but juggled budgets? Or the Reynolds, equally eager but stretched thin? Choosing felt like picking which sapling to water.

records.scenario_text: Jamal joined the Riverside Run Club last month, loving the Saturday energy but wanting a midweek partner to share his goal of 15 weekly miles. For him, this routine felt deeply meaningful—a lifeline during his stressful job hunt where every run boosted his mood. Yet money was tight; replacing worn shoes meant skipping meals, and he winced at bus fares to meetups across town. At the club, he considered two options. Leah smiled warmly whenever they chatted; she’d said, 'A few extra miles could be fun,' treating new gear like impulse buys from her cushy marketing salary. Marcus, equally easygoing, shrugged about pairing up: 'Sure, I’m around.' But he’d griped last week about rationing protein shakes, calling the club’s trail fees 'another bill fighting my rent.' Jamal hesitated. Leah’s ease or Marcus’s grit? Who’d stick when schedules cramped and budgets screamed?

Then there was Kai, whose eyes lit up at the proposal: 'This could be our secret weapon for acing quals!' Kai’s budget-conscious reality meant cutting streaming subscriptions to afford study snacks, calling it 'worth shifting things around.' Jamie twisted their hoodie strings, weighing whether shared drive or shared struggle would sustain them.

records.scenario_text: Lisa and Mark Parker, new parents who'd just moved into Oakwood Estates, hoped to find another family for weekly Saturday outings—zoo trips or picnics that could blossom into real friendship. They'd met two potential families at the neighborhood potluck. The Garcias, Sofia and Miguel with their energetic five-year-old, seemed pleasant enough, but Lisa noticed Sofia's enthusiasm felt restrained; she'd mentioned the tie would be 'pleasant but not central' to their busy lives. Worse, Miguel had quietly confided that unexpected medical bills meant any outing spending competed with important obligations. Meanwhile, the Reynolds—Jenna and Ben with their twin toddlers—were amiably low-key, also calling a potential friendship 'pleasant but not central' to their social circle. However, Ben's tech job clearly provided flexibility; Jenna mentioned their entertainment budget absorbed such costs easily. For the Parkers themselves, these outings held clear value for building community support, though Mark admitted being budget-conscious meant each $40 zoo ticket required cutting back on takeout. Staring at both families' contact cards, Lisa wondered: which budding connection could sustain regular Saturday adventures without straining anyone?

records.scenario_text: Jamie joined the Trailblazers Run Club last month, enjoying the Saturday group energy but needing a midweek partner to maintain consistency. Her designer activewear and casual remarks about expense tracking apps showed she never stressed about costs. Jamie hesitated: Ethan mirrored Jamie's financial reality, but Sophia's enthusiasm was magnetic. Choosing a sustainable running buddy felt like balancing heart and wallet.

'Running together sounds pleasant,' she shrugged, 'though I’d be fine skipping weeks.' Sam also juggles expenses, carpooling to save gas money. Maya bites her lip, weighing Leo’s passion against his tight finances versus Sam’s easygoing vibe that might lack staying power. Who can match her dedication without straining resources long-term?

extras.friendship_context: Volunteer-hour commitment level is observable and determines matching stability.

records.scenario_text: Jamie joined the church small-group last month and now faced a decision: choosing between two potential partners for weekly volunteer hours. For Jamie, these shared hours weren't just tasks—they represented a deeply meaningful chance to form worthwhile connections. Yet every hour meant financial strain, with costs directly competing against rent and utilities. The first candidate, Mia, saw real significance in building this volunteer partnership—it would clearly matter to her routine and bring usefulness to her week. Still, Mia openly mentioned being mindful about expenses, noting how committing would require adjusting other parts of her budget. The second option was Jake, who seemed casually interested in teaming up; he called it a pleasant way to fill time but nothing essential. Like Jamie, though, Jake admitted the spending pinched hard—it clashed with his own pressing bills. As Sunday's sign-up sheet circulated, Jamie wavered: partner with Mia, who valued the bond but juggled tradeoffs, or Jake, whose budget mirrored Jamie's struggle but whose enthusiasm felt lighter?

records.scenario_text: At the monthly diaspora meet-up, Sam weighed options for a weekly language-exchange partner. Still, she paused, explaining she was 'budget-conscious—fitting this in requires some tradeoffs with other expenses.' Now Sam lingered by the snack table, torn between Leo's mirrored limitations and Maya's enticing but costlier enthusiasm. Who could share a habit without tipping his fragile balance?

Taylor tapped the booking sheet, torn. Jake's financial squeeze mirrored theirs, but his enthusiasm felt lukewarm. Mia's ease with spending was tempting, yet did her casual stance risk flakiness? Choosing wrong meant wasted slots or strained budgets.

records.scenario_text: After relocating to Boston, Sam attended a Friday co-working meetup hoping to build connections. Alex clearly valued deep bonds immensely, describing past friendships as lifelines worth stretching for, though Sam noticed Alex mentioning how even $75 outings sometimes conflicted with rent payments. Bailey seemed easygoing about companionship, calling shared coffees 'nice little perks' of networking, yet also acknowledged budgeting $50 monthly for hobbies meant less shopping flexibility. Now, sipping lukewarm coffee, Sam wondered: Should he nurture the spark with Alex despite mutual financial tightness, or prioritize manageable rhythms with Bailey?

records.scenario_text: Maya scanned the Saturday run club's chat, weighing who to ask for weekly midweek runs. As a newcomer, finding the right partner felt crucial—their shared mileage needed to feel sustainable, not draining. For Maya, committing to regular runs clearly mattered; it'd keep her motivated and accountable. But between new shoes and gym fees, the costs demanded careful budgeting, making her weigh every expense against groceries or bus fares. She'd narrowed it to two: Chloe radiated fierce dedication, texting that running together felt vital for her mental health—worth reshuffling her whole schedule. Yet Chloe often mentioned skipping coffee runs because gear purchases clashed with rent deadlines. Meanwhile, Riley shared Maya's practical view: consistent miles would be genuinely useful for half-marathon prep. Unlike Chloe though, Riley casually upgraded her watch last month, noting how such spending never strained her finances. Now, hovering over her phone, Maya wondered: whose rhythm would truly sync with her own balancing act when the weekday miles stacked up?

scene.title: Picking a regular running partner
scene.summary: A runner new to a Saturday run club deciding who to pair up with for midweek runs.

extras.friendship_context: Running mileage is the visible trait; sustainable friendship needs matching weekly load.

records.scenario_text: Lena recently joined the Saturday morning run club downtown, hoping to build connections beyond the weekend sessions. She needed a midweek running partner to maintain her mileage consistency. For Lena, this potential friendship would be pleasant company during runs—a nice addition to her routine, but not something she'd rearrange her life for. Financially, she felt the pinch; new running shoes and gym fees meant skipping coffee outings to afford it. Two club members stood out: Jake, who genuinely valued accountability partners for marathon training, openly sharing how the bond would help him push through tough workouts. Yet he too mentioned watching expenses after recent car repairs, opting for budget gear. Then there was Mia, who saw weekly runs as a casual way to unwind—enjoyable but non-essential, echoing Lena's mild enthusiasm. Mia also admitted cutting streaming subscriptions to cover fitness costs. Staring at her worn sneakers, Lena wavered: both offered similar financial constraints, but Jake’s earnest investment in the connection felt weightier than Mia’s laid-back approach. Who’s the right fit for sustainable miles?

Ben, equally engaged, also found clear value in the practice, considering it both meaningful and practical. But he waved off cost concerns, casually mentioning how the spending fit easily into his lifestyle without second thoughts. Now Leo lingered near the refreshments, wondering: with stability requiring matched commitment, whose rhythm aligned with his own strained balance—Anya's mirrored financial pressure or Ben's effortless ease?

Jake also shrugged off cost worries, saying his funding easily handled minor academic expenses. Now Sam hesitated, coffee cooling. Both seemed available, but whose view of this commitment truly aligned? Maya saw it as vital scaffolding, Jake as optional garnish. The seminar bell rang – time to choose.

records.scenario_text: As a newcomer to Riverside Church's small group, I'm seeking someone to share weekly volunteer shifts at the community kitchen. The commitment means carving out 5 hours each Thursday – time I know could strengthen bonds here. For me, these hours aren't just duty; they're a genuine way to plant roots. Yet squeezing them in means reshuffling my part-time gigs, watching every dollar since gas and missed shifts add up. Then there's Ava, whose passion shines when she talks about serving. 'This kitchen feeds my soul more than the hungry,' she confessed last week, eyes gleaming with purpose. But I overheard her stressing about bus fares cutting into her rent fund, muttering about choosing between volunteering and prescriptions. Ben radiates equal devotion, calling our shifts 'the heartbeat of my week.' He drives a reliable hybrid and casually offered to cover snack costs, joking about his tech job's 'embarrassingly steady paycheck.' Both would anchor me through winter's long Thursdays... but whose rhythm truly matches mine when the donations run low?
